questionText: The Government of India Act of 1919 provided for the:
options: a) Association of Indians with the executive council of the Viceroy
b) Separation of Provincial and Central budgets for the first time
c) Establishment of the Joint Public Service Commission
d) Establishment of an All-India Federation
explanation:
The Government of India Act of 1919 was based on the Montagu-Chelmsford Reforms. The British government for the first time in August 1917 declared that its objective was to gradually introduce responsible government in India, but as an integral part of the British Empire.
Under the 1919 Act, the Indian Legislative Council at the Centre was replaced by a bicameral system consisting of a Council of State (Upper House) and a Legislative Assembly (Lower House). This Act for the first time separated the provincial and central budgets, with provincial legislatures being authorised to make their budgets. Hence, option b is the correct.
At the provincial level, the diarchy was introduced and the subjects were further divided into reserved and transferred subjects. The reserved subjects were in the charge of councillors, who were nominated by the Governor. The transferred subjects were in charge of councillors, who were appointed by the Governor.
The Government of India Act of 1909 provided (for the first time) for the association of Indians with the executive councils of the Viceroy and Governors. Satyendra Prasad Sinha became the first Indian to join the Viceroy’s executive council. He was appointed as the Law Member.
The Government of India Act of 1935 contemplated the establishment of an All-India Federation in which Governors’ Provinces and the Chief Commissioners’ Provinces and those Indian states which might accede to be united were to be included. It also provided for provincial autonomy and the establishment of not only a Federal Public Service Commission but also a Provincial Public Service Commission and Joint Public Service Commission for two or more provinces.
Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
Consider the following statements.
1. C. Rajagopalachari was the President of the Indian National Congress (INC) at the time of India’s independence.
2. Dadabhai Naoroji is the only person to preside over three INC sessions before independence.
3. The only session of the INC where Mahatma Gandhi presided was the Belgaum session of 1924.
How many of the above statements are correct?
explanation:
Acharya J.B. Kripalani presided over the Indian National Congress (INC) from 1946 to 1948, that is, at the time of independence of India. He was succeeded by Pattabhi Sitaramayya. C. Rajagopalachari never presided over any of the INC annual sessions. So, statement 1 is not correct.
Dadabhai Naoroji presided over 3 congress sessions, all of them before independence: the Calcutta Session (1886), the Lahore Session (1893) and the Calcutta Session (1906). Jawahar Lal Nehru presided over 3 congress sessions before Independence: Lahore Session (1929), Lucknow Session (1936) and Faizpur Session (1937). He presided over three more congress sessions after independence in (1951, 1953 and 1954). So, statement 2 is not correct.
Mahatma Gandhi presided over the Belgaum session of 1924. This was the only time he presided over any INC session. So, statement 3 is correct.
So, only one of the above statements is correct.
Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
Consider the following activities:
1. Promotion of the use of charkha and khadi
2. Establishment of National schools and colleges
3. Boycott of foreign cloth and liquor
4. Potrayal of India as land belonging to the Hindus
How many of the above methods of struggle were adopted by ‘No-Changers’ within the Indian National Congress during the 1920s?
explanation:
No-Changers:
After the suspension of the Non-Cooperation Movement, one section led by C.R. Das, Motilal Nehru and Ajmal Khan came to be known as the ‘Swarajists while the other school of thought led by C. Rajagopalachari, Vallabhbhai Patel, Rajendra Prasad and M.A. Ansari came to be known as the ‘No Changers’.
‘The No-Changers devoted themselves to constructive work that included:
Popularisation of the use of charkha and khadi. So, point 1 is correct.
National schools and colleges were set up. So, point 2 is correct.
Removing untouchability
Boycott of foreign cloth and liquor. So, point 3 is correct.
Promotion of Hindu-Muslim unity. So, point 4 is not correct.
So, only three of the above methods of struggle were adopted by ‘No-Changers’ within the Indian National Congress during the 1920s.
Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
Consider the following objectives:
1. Form a democratic and nationalist movement
2. Promote friendly relations among nationalist political workers
3. Struggle for complete independence from the colonial rule
4. Develop and propagate an anti-colonial nationalist ideology
5. Unifying the people over a common economic and political programme
How many of the above were the objectives of the Indian National Congress in its early years of formation in the 19th century?
explanation:
The main aims of the Indian National Congress (INC) in the initial stage were to:
form a democratic, nationalist movement. So, point 1 is correct.
politicise and politically educate people;
promote friendly relations among nationalist political workers from different parts of the country. So, point 2 is correct.
establish the headquarters for a movement;
develop and propagate an anti-colonial nationalist ideology. So, point 4 is correct.
formulate and present popular demands before the government with a view to unifying the people over a common economic and political programme. So, point 5 is correct.
develop and consolidate a feeling of national unity among people irrespective of religion, caste or province;
carefully promote and nurture Indian nationhood.
The INC declared Purna Swaraj or Complete Independence for India as its goal for the first time at the Lahore Session in 1929. It was not their aim in their initial days. So, point 3 is not correct.
So, only four of the above were the objectives of the Indian National Congress in its early years of formation in the 19th century.
Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText: Which one of the following was the originator of the Drain of Wealth theory?
explanation:
In the 1870s major economic critiques of the colonial rule on poverty problems were Dadabhai Naoroji, M.G. Ranade, Romesh Chunder Dutt, Gopal Krishna Gokhale, G. Subramaniya Iyer, G.V. Joshi, Bal Gangadhar Tilak and Surendranath Banerjea.
Dadabhai Naoroji authored the book Poverty and Un-British Rule in India (1901) which remains a significant milestone in the formulation of the nationalist political economy. He was the originator of the ‘drain of wealth’ theory. He criticised Britain for ‘ignoring India’s interests, and making it the drudge for the benefit of England’. In 1870, he also wrote a book titled ‘The Poverty of India’.
Mahadev Govind Ranade’s Essays on Indian Economics (1896), Romesh Chunder Dutt’s two-volume Economic History of India (1902) and Subrahmanya Iyer’s Some Economic Aspects of British Rule in India (1903), among several others, held colonial rule guilty of India’s growing poverty caused by a prolonged and constant ‘drain of wealth’ from India to England.
Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
Consider the following leaders:
1. Prithwishchandra Ray
2. Raj Narayan Bose
3. Badruddin Tyabji
4. Madan Mohan Malviya
5. Ashwini Kumar Dutt
How many of the above leaders belonged to the extremist school of thought during the freedom struggle in India?
explanation:
The Extremist leaders in the early twentieth century aimed at getting Swaraj to end the tyrannical rule of the British. The leaders belonging to this school of thought included Bal Gangadhar Tilak, Lala Lajpat Rai, Ajit Singh, Bipin Chandra Pal, Aurobindo Ghose, Raj Narayan Bose, Ashwini Kumar Dutt, Syed Haider Raza and Chidambaram Pillai. So, points 2 and 5 are correct.
In the period 1903-1905, moderate leadership was provided by men like Surendranath Banerjea, K.K. Mitra, Prithwishchandra Ray, A.O. Hume, W.C. Banerjee, Dadabhai Naoroji, Feroze Shah Mehta, Gopal Krishna Gokhale, Pandit Madan Mohan Malaviya, Badruddin Tyabji, Justice Ranade and G. Subramanya Aiyar. So, points 1, 3 and 4 are not correct.
So, only two of the above leaders belonged to the extremist school of thought during the freedom struggle in India.
Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText: Which one of the following was the significance of the Benares Session of 1905?
options: a) Partition of Bengal was condemned
b) Vande Mataram was sung for the first time
c) Swaraj was mentioned for the first time
d) First session to be Presided by a Muslim member
explanation:
Benares Session of 1905 was presided over by Gopal Krishna Gokhale. The Indian National Congress resolved to condemn the partition of Bengal and the reactionary policies of Curzon and support the anti-partition and Swadeshi Movement of Bengal.
Calcutta Session of 1906 was presided over by Dadabhai Naoroji. It was in this session the word ‘Swaraj’ was mentioned for the first time. The self-government or swaraj to be the goal of the Congress was declared at this session. Other resolutions included a resolution on the Partition of Bengal, a resolution on Swadeshi and a resolution on Boycott.
Calcutta Session of 1896 was presided over by Rahimtullah M. Sayani. In this session, the National song ‘Vande Mataram’ was sung for the first time.
Madras Session of 1887 was the first session presided over by a Muslim member. It was presided over by Badruddin Tyabji.
Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference to the ‘Swadeshi’ movement:
1. The painting of Bharatmata by Raja Ravi Varma was used to invoke nationalism during the Swadeshi Movement.
2. It spread to Chennai under the leadership of Chidambaram Pillai.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation:
The painting of ‘Bharatmata’ was created by Abanindranath Tagore during the Swadeshi movement. It depicts the nationalist icon of the stature of the Goddess, yet it is distinct from any known deity of the Hindu pantheon. So, statement 1 is not correct.
The movement spread to other parts of the country—in Poona and Bombay under Tilak, in Punjab under Lala Lajpat Rai and Ajit Singh, in Delhi under Syed Haider Raza, and in Madras under Chidambaram Pillai. So, statement 2 is correct.
Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
With reference to the contributions made by Nandalal Bose in modern Indian history, consider the following statements:
1. He was an ardent disciple of Abanindranath Tagore.
2. He was the first recipient of a scholarship offered by the Indian Society of Oriental Art.
3. He is credited to have handwritten the original copy of the Constitution of India.
4. He decorated the Mandapa of the Haripura council of the Indian National Congress session in 1937.
How many of the above statements are correct?
explanation:
Nandalal Bose (1882-1966) is considered to be one of the pioneers of modern Indian art. He was an ardent disciple of Abanindranath Tagore, the leader of the revivalist movement in Indian art. Nandalal Bose left a major imprint on Indian art and was the first recipient of a scholarship offered by the Indian Society of Oriental Art, founded in 1907. So, statements 1 and 2 are correct.
When the Indian Constitution was being drafted, Nandalal Bose was entrusted with the task of illustrating the pages. Along with his disciple Rammanohar, Nandalal Bose took up the task of beautifying and decorating the original manuscript of the Constitution. He was also responsible for the design of Indian government awards like Bharat Ratna, Padmabhushan, Padmashri etc. The Constitution of India was handwritten by calligrapher Prem Behari Narain Raizada. So, statement 3 is not correct.
In 1937 Nandalal decorated the Mandapa of the Haripura council of the Indian National Congress with 83 pieces of paintings which were a fusion of the Bengali folk painting and his own graceful style. So, statement 4 is correct.
So, only three of the above statements are correct.
Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.
Knowledge Box
Nandalal Bose’s more known paintings include Savitri and Jama, Gandhari, Shiddharta with the wounded goose, Shiva drinking halahal, Meerabai, Birth of Shri Chaitanya etc.
Nandalal pioneered the revival of mural painting in India and two of his best works can be seen in Kirti Mandir in Baroda and in the Chinabhavan in Shantiniketan.
As India won independence, the Constituent Assembly decided on the Sarnath pillar as the national emblem. On January 26, 1950, the Lion Capital of Asoka at Sarnath officially became the national emblem of India. Five students of renowned artist Nandalal Bose created the emblem. Among them were Jagdish Mittal, Kripal Singh Shekhawat, Gauri Bhanja and Dinanath Bhargava.
Relevance: There is an ongoing controversy regarding the National Emblem placed in the new Parliament Building of India.
subject: History

questionText:
Consider the following reasons:
1. The differences concerning the leadership of the Congress session
2. The extension of the Swadeshi movement in the future
3. Location of the session
How many of the above reasons were responsible for the Surat Split among the Indian nationalists during the freedom struggle?
explanation:
The Surat Split was the splitting of the Indian National Congress into two groups - the Moderates and Extremists - at the Surat session in 1907.
In 1907, as opposed to the demands of the Extremists who wanted either Lala Lajpat Rai or Bal Gangadhar Tilak to be elected as President, Rasa Behari Ghosh was chosen as President by the Moderates. This finalised the split within the Congress. So, point 1 is correct.
The Extremists wanted to extend the Boycott and Swadeshi Movement to regions outside Bengal and include all forms of associations (such as government service, law courts, legislative councils, etc.) within the boycott programme and thus start a nationwide mass movement. The Extremists also wanted a reiteration of the Swadeshi, boycott and national education resolutions. The Moderates sought to drop the resolutions on Swadeshi, boycott and national education. So, point 2 is correct.
The Extremists wanted the 1907 session to be held in Nagpur (Central Provinces) with Tilak or Lajpat Rai as the president. The Moderates wanted the session at Surat to exclude Tilak from the presidency since a leader from the host province could not be session president (Surat being in Tilak’s home province of Bombay). So, point 3 is correct.
So, all three of the above reasons were responsible for the Surat Split among the Indian nationalists during the freedom struggle.
Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
With reference to the Bal Gangadhar Tilak, consider the following statements:
1. He was the first person to be convicted of sedition in colonial India.
2. He was charged for exciting disaffection through an article he published in his weekly Kesari.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation:
Bal Gangadhar Tilak was the first person to be convicted of sedition in colonial India. Tilak faced three trials in cases related to sedition (1897, 1909 and 1916) and was imprisoned twice. So, statement 1 is correct.
In 1897, he was charged with sedition for exciting disaffection through an article he published in his weekly Kesari and was sentenced to 12 months imprisonment. So, statement 2 is correct.
In 1909, he was tried for a second time for sedition for what he had written in 1908 in his Kesari about a bomb thrown by Bengal revolutionaries in Muzaffarpur, resulting in the death of two innocent European women. He was represented by MA Jinnah, but his bail application was rejected. He was sent to Mandalay (Burma) jail for six years.
It was proposed to prosecute him for sedition for the third time because of speeches delivered by Tilak in Marathi under the auspices of the Historical Research Society at Belgaum and later at Ahmednagar in 1916 on the subject of Home Rule.
Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference to Morley-Minto reforms of 1909:
1. It gave the members the right to vote on separate items in the budget and move resolutions on the budget.
2. It provided for separate representation of chambers of commerce and presidency corporations.
3. It provided limited suffrage to women property holders.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation:
The Morley-Minto reforms (1909) enlarged the deliberative functions of the legislative councils at both the Central and Provincial levels. The legislatures are now allowed to pass resolutions, ask questions and supplementary, vote on separate items in the budget and move resolutions on the budget. So, statement 1 is correct.
It also provided separate representation of presidency corporations, chambers of commerce, universities and zamindars. So, statement 2 is correct.
The Montagu-Chelmsford reforms of 1919 provided limited suffrage to women property holders but it did not give women the right to contest elections. In Britain itself, women got the right to vote only in 1918. The Government of India Act of 1919 allowed the provincial council to decide whether women could vote provided they met stringent property, income or educational criteria. Between the years 1919 and 1929, all of the British provinces and the princely states granted women the right to vote. So, statement 3 is not correct.
Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
With reference to the Barisal Conference in Bengal, consider the following statements:
1. It was organized to protest against the Chauri Chaura incident.
2. It was presided over by Barrister Abdul Rasul.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation:
A political conference was held at Barisal in 1906. It was especially important because of the partition of Bengal and also because the Lieutenant Governor of Bengal Andrew Henderson Leith Fraser had started the repression policy. So, statement 1 is not correct.
The President of the conference was Barrister Abdul Rasul. The heroic boy Chittaranjan Guha Thukarta was brutally assaulted for shouting “Bande Mataram”. The police forcibly dispersed the 1906 Barisal Conference and brutally beat up a large number of the participants. At the conference, a brutal lathi charge was made by the police on the delegates and volunteers and "Bande Mataram" badges worn by them were violently torn off. So, statement 2 is correct.
Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
Which of the following personalities were associated with the famous Delhi Conspiracy Case of 1912?
1. Rash Behari Ghosh
2. Aurobindo Ghosh
3. Sachin Sanyal
4. Lala Hardayal
5. Amir Chand
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
explanation:
Delhi Conspiracy Case or Delhi-Lahore conspiracy case (1912) was associated with a death attempt made on Lord Hardinge, the then Viceroy of India. A custom-made bomb was tossed into the emissary's howdah (elephant carriage) during a stately parade in Delhi. The event was the exchange of the British capital from Calcutta to Delhi.
On 23rd December 1912, Rash Behari Bose and Sachin Sanyal organized a daring attempt and a bomb was thrown on Viceroy Lord Hardinge in Chandni Chowk, Delhi, killing his attendants. Thirteen people were arrested and tried in the case.
Dina Nath became a government approver under pressure. Four persons were convicted and executed for their roles in the conspiracy including Amir Chand, Awadh Bihari, Bal Mukund and Basant Kumar Biswas. Rash Behari Bose (and not Rash Behari Ghosh) who was the person behind the plan evaded arrest as he fled to Japan. So, point 1 is not correct and points 3 and 5 are correct.
Aurobindo Ghosh and Lala Hardayal were not associated with the famous Delhi Conspiracy Case of 1912. So, points 2 and 4 are not correct.
Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference to the Jugantar Party:
1. It was established in 1906 with Prafulla Chaki and Khudiram Bose as its founders.
2. It had plans to free India from British domination with acts of violence.
3. It organized a series of dacoities to collect funds for the Indo-German conspiracy.
How many of the above statements are correct?
explanation:
Jugantar Party was the leading revolutionary terrorist group in colonial Bengal formed by the inner circle of Anushilan Samiti. Barindra Kumar Ghosh (younger brother of Aurobindo Ghosh) and Bagha Jatin were its main leaders. It was established in 1906 and was headquartered at Baubazar Street in Calcutta. So, statement 1 is not correct.
The Party vowed to free India from British colonial domination with religious inspiration tempered by acts of violence, justifying murders by Ksatriyas to further the happiness of mankind. So, statement 2 is correct.
The first Taxicab dacoity took place on February 12, 1915, in Garden Reach, Kolkata under the leadership of Narendra Bhattacharya. On different other occasions, similar kinds of dacoities were conducted in various parts of Calcutta to collect funds for the Indo-German conspiracy. So, statement 3 is correct.
In 1908, Hemchandra Qanungo, a member of the Jugantar Party, set up a combined religious school and bomb factory at a garden house in the Maniktala suburb of Calcutta. Hemchandra Qanungo went abroad to get both political and military training. He obtained the training from a Russian Emigrant in Paris.
So, only two of the above statements are correct.
Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
Consider the following statements:
1. Lala Har Dayal founded the Ghadar party with its headquarters in San Francisco.
2. Bhupendranath Dutta was associated with the Berlin Indian Independence Committee.
3. Shyamji Krishnavarma published a monthly journal ‘The Indian Sociologist’.
4. Indian Home Rule Society in London was started by V.D Savarkar and Madanlal Dhingra.
How many of the above statements are correct?
explanation:
In November 1913, the Ghadar party was founded by Lala Har Dayal with active support from Ram Chandra, Barakatullah and Bhagwan Singh. It was a revolutionary group organized around a weekly newspaper ‘The Ghadar’ with its headquarters in San Francisco and branches along the US coast and in the Far East. So, statement 1 is correct.
During World War I, the Berlin Committee for Indian Independence was established in 1915 by Virendranath Chattopadhyay, Bhupendranath Dutta, Lala Hardayal and others. The objectives of the committee included inciting rebellion among the troops, supplying explosives to Indian revolutionaries and even organizing an invasion of British India. So, statement 2 is correct.
Shyamji Krishnavarma published a monthly journal ‘The Indian Sociologist’ which was a noted platform for anti-colonial views and was banned in India as ‘seditious literature’. So, statement 3 is correct.
The Indian Home Rule Society was started in London in 1905 by Shyamji Krishnavarma with support from Bhikaji Cama and Dadabai Naoroji to serve as a residence for Indian students and promote nationalist views. Revolutionaries such as VD Savarkar, Har Dayal and Madanlal Dhingra also became members of the society. So, statement 4 is not correct.
So, only three of the above statements are correct.
Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText: With reference to the Indian national movement, ‘Swadesh Sevak Home’ and ‘United India House’ were known for:
options: a) their organization of revolutionary groups in the United States of America
b) inciting rebellion among the troops during World War I
c) their support for the Swadeshi Movement
d) instigating an armed revolt by disrupting communication lines
explanation:
The Ghadar Party was a revolutionary group organized with its headquarters in San Francisco. It included ex-soldiers who had migrated from Punjab to the USA and Canada in search of better employment opportunities.
To carry out pre-Ghadar revolutionary activities, Swadesh Sevak Home was set up by GD Kumar in Vancouver along the lines of India House in London. The ‘United India House’ was founded by Tarak Nath and G.D. Kumar in Seattle for the weekly meeting of Indian labourers.
Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
Consider the following statements with respect to the Home Rule League set up by Annie Besant:
1. It worked mainly in Madras covering the rest of India including Bombay City.
2. New India and Commonweal newspapers were published to demand self-rule.
3. Annie Besant succeeded in getting Congress to approve her scheme of Home Rule Leagues.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation:
Annie Besant set up her All-India Home Rule league in 1916 in Madras covering the rest of India (including Bombay city). George Arundale was the organizing secretary of the league. Besides Arundale, the main work was done by B.W Wadia and C.P Ramaswamy Aiyar. The Home Rule League set up by Tilak was restricted to Maharashtra (excluding Bombay city), Karnataka, Central Provinces and Berar. So, statement 1 is correct.
Annie Besant along with her lieutenants- Arundale, CP Ramaswamy Aiyar and BL Wadia brought out newspapers ‘New India’ and ‘Commonweal’. These were important means used by Besant to explain the demand for self-rule. So, statement 2 is correct.
Annie Besant failed to get Congress to approve her scheme of Home Rule Leagues, the Congress did commit itself to a programme of educative propaganda and to a revival of local-level Congress committees. Not willing to wait for too long, Besant laid the condition that if the Congress did not implement its commitments, she would be free to set up her own league—which she finally had to, as there was no response from the Congress. So, statement 3 is not correct.
Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText: Who among the following suggested that the Congress ‘was a product of Lord Dufferin’s brain,’ and it was started with the object of saving the British Empire from danger than with that of winning political liberty for India?
explanation: In his Young India published in 1916, the Extremist leader Lala Lajpat Rai used the safety-valve theory to attack the Moderates in the Congress. Having discussed the theory at length and suggested that the Congress ‘was a product of Lord Dufferin’s brain,’ he argued that ‘the Congress was started more with the object of saving the British Empire from danger than with that of winning political liberty for India. The interests of the British Empire were primary and those of India only secondary’. Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText: ‘The August Declaration’ of 1917 is known for:
options: a) Announcing the complete withdrawal of British troops from India
b) Granting full autonomy to princely states in India
c) Indicating to bring a responsible government in India
d) Pledging full independence for India within a specified timeframe
explanation:
The Secretary of State for India, Edwin Samuel Montagu, made a statement on August 20, 1917, in the British House of Commons in what has come to be known as the August Declaration of 1917. The statement said: “The government policy is of an increasing participation of Indians in every branch of administration and gradual development of self-governing institutions with a view to the progressive realisation of responsible government in India.”
From there onwards, the demand by nationalists for self-government or home rule could not be termed as seditious since the attainment of self-government for Indians now became a government policy, unlike Morley’s statement in 1909 that the reforms were not intended to give self-government to India. Also, the use of the term ‘responsible government’ implied the condition that the rulers were to be answerable to the elected representatives and not only to the imperial government in London.
Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
Consider the following personalities:
1. George Yule
2. Henry Cotton
3. Sarojini Naidu
4. Annie Besant
5. Subhash Chandra Bose
How many of the above served as the President for only one annual session of the Indian National Congress?
explanation:
Important Indian National Congress Annual Sessions and its Presidents.
Year President Place
1888 George Yule Allahabad. So, point 1 is correct.
1904 Henry Cotton Bombay. So, point 2 is correct.
1925 Sarojini Naidu Kanpur. So, point 3 is correct.
1917 Annie Besant Calcutta. So, point 4 is correct.
1938 and 1939 Subhash Chandra Bose Haripura and Tripuri. So, point 5 is not correct.
So, only four of the above served as the President for only one annual session of the Indian National Congress.
Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
Which of the following demands were made in the ‘Lucknow Pact’?
1. The term of the legislative council should be five years
2. The government should declare self-government on Indians at an early date
3. The salaries of the Secretary of State for India should not drawn from Indian funds
4. Half the members of the Provincial Executive Councils should be Indians.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
explanation:
The Lucknow Pact between the Congress and the Muslim League could be considered an important event in the course of the nationalistic struggle for freedom. The pact was signed in December 1916 at a joint session of both parties in Lucknow.
The joint demands were:
The term of the legislative council should be five years. So, point 1 is correct.
The government should declare that it would confer self-government on Indians at an early date. So, point 2 is correct.
The representative assemblies at the central as well as provincial level should be further expanded with an elected majority and more powers given to them
The salaries of the Secretary of State for India should be paid by the British treasury and not drawn from Indian funds. So, point 3 is correct.
Half the members of the viceroy’s and provincial governors’ executive councils should be Indians. So, point 4 is correct.
Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference to the Hindustan Republican Association (HRA):
1. It was founded by Bhagat Singh and Rajguru.
2. It advocated nationalization of large-scale industries and railways.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation:
The Hindustan Republican Association (HRA) was founded in October 1924 in Kanpur by Ramprasad Bismil, Jogesh Chandra Chatterjee and Sachin Sanyal, with an aim to organise an armed revolution to overthrow the colonial government and establish in its place the Federal Republic of United States of India whose basic principle would be adult franchise. So, statement 1 is not correct.
The association envisaged that the “basic principle of the republic shall be universal suffrage and the abolition of all systems which make any kind of exploitation of man by man possible.”
HRA’s founding council, in its meeting in October 1924, had decided to preach social revolutionary and communist principles.
It was committed to “the organisation of labour and kisans” as it was necessary for the successful struggle against capitalism and feudalism.
The HRA did believe and acted according to the methods of older revolutionaries. It also believed in the armed overthrow of the imperialist government.
It advocated the nationalization of the railways and large-scale industries such as steel, ship-building and mines. So, statements 2 is correct.
Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText: Which one of the following was the leader of Ramoshi Peasant Force?
explanation:
The first of the revolutionary activities in Maharashtra was the organisation of the Ramoshi Peasant Force by Vasudev Balwant Phadke in 1879, which aimed to rid the country of the British by instigating an armed revolt by disrupting communication lines. It hoped to raise funds for its activities through dacoities. It was suppressed prematurely.
Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
Consider the following statement regarding the Manicktolla bomb conspiracy of 1908:
1. Members of the the Anushilan Samiti were arrested in this case.
2. Aurobindo Ghosh and Barindra Ghosh were charged with waging war against the King.
3. Tej Bahadur Sapru acted as the defence counsel for the accused revolutionaries in the case.
How many of the above statements are correct?
explanation:
This legal proceeding involved the trial of several Indian nationalists associated with the Anushilan Samiti in Calcutta. Members of the Anushilan group were arrested including the Ghosh brothers, Aurobindo and Barindra, who were tried in the Alipore conspiracy case, variously called Manicktolla bomb conspiracy or Muraripukur conspiracy. (Barindra Ghosh’s house was on Muraripukur Road in the Manicktolla suburb of Calcutta). Aurobindo Ghosh and Barindra Ghosh were charged with ‘conspiracy’ or ‘waging war against the King’ – the equivalent of high treason and punishable with death by hanging. So, statements 1 and 2 are correct.
Chittaranjan Das was the defence counsel for the accused revolutionaries during the case. Aurobindo was acquitted of all charges with the judge condemning the flimsy nature of the evidence against him. Barindra Ghosh, the head of the secret society of revolutionaries and Ullaskar Dutt, the maker of the bombs, were given the death penalty which was later commuted to life in prison. So, statement 3 is not correct.
So, only two of the above statements are correct.
Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
With reference to the Nationalist’s response to the First World War, consider the following statements:
1. Both Moderates and Muslim League supported the British Empire in its war efforts and demanded self-rule at the end of the War.
2. The Extremists along with the Revolutionaries wanted to take advantage of the war situation to overthrow the Britishers.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation:
Moderates and extremist groups within the Congress came together with the Lucknow pact of 1916, after their split in 1907 Surat. They decided to support the British Empire during the war and demanded self-rule at the end of the war. Along with the Congress, the Muslim League and other regional parties also extended their support. So, statement 1 is correct.
While extremists supported the British efforts, the revolutionaries decided to take advantage of the situation to overthrow the British. Consequently, the Ghadar party was established in North America. Later they launched many movements in Punjab, Bengal etc. involving armed rebellion against the British. So, statement 2 is not correct.
Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
With reference to ‘Indian Statutory Commission,’ consider the following statements:
1. It was appointed to report on the working of the Montagu-Chelmsford Reforms.
2. The Unionist Party in Punjab and the Justice Party decided to boycott the Commission.
3. The Bombay Legislative Council appointed Chimanlal Harilal Setalvad to work with the Commission.
How many of the above statements are correct?
explanation:
Based on the ‘Montagu-Chelmsford Reforms’ the Government of India Act, 1919, was enacted. According to this act, after 10 years, a commission would be appointed to study the progress of the Constitutional scheme and to suggest new steps. Hence in 1927, the Indian Statutory Commission (Simon Commission) was appointed by the British Government to recommend to the Government whether India is ready for further constitutional reforms and along what lines. So, statement 1 is correct.
The Muslim League supported the Simon Commission in its Lahore Session (1927) under the chairmanship of Muhammad Shafi. The Simon Commission was not boycotted by the Unionist Party in Punjab and the Justice Party in South India. The Bombay Legislative Council appointed Dr. B.R. Ambedkar to work with the Simon Commission. So, statements 2 and 3 are not correct.
So, only one of the above statements is correct.
Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.
subject: History
topic: British Administration and Policies in India

questionText:
With reference to the Home Rule Movement, consider the following statements:
1. Muhammad Ali Jinnah led the Bombay division of the League.
2. Mahatma Gandhi renounced his title of Kaiser-i-Hind during this movement.
3. It influenced the August 1917 declaration of Montague.
How many of the above statements are correct?
explanation:
The Home Rule Movement was the Indian response to the First World War. All India Home Rule League was to be along the lines of the Irish Home Rule League.
The Prominent leaders of this league were Bal Gangadhar Tilak, Annie Besant, G.S. Khaparde, Sir S. Subramania Iyer, Joseph Baptista and Muhammad Ali Jinnah. The Home Rule agitation was later joined by Motilal Nehru, Jawaharlal Nehru, Chittaranjan Das, Madan Mohan Malaviya, Tej Bahadur Sapru and Lala Lajpat Rai. Some of these leaders became heads of local branches of the League. Muhammad Ali Jinnah led the Bombay division. So, statement 1 is correct.
The government came down with severe repression against the Home Rule League Movement, especially in the Madras division. This invited nationwide protests. In a dramatic gesture, Sir S. Subramania Aiyar renounced his knighthood.
Gandhi renounced his title of Kaiser-i-Hind after the Jallianwala Bagh massacre. So, statement 2 is not correct.
Home Rule agitation influenced the August 1917 declaration of Montagu and the Montague Chelmsford reforms. So, statement 3 is correct.
So, only two of the above statements are correct.
Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
With reference to the sessions of the Indian National Congress (INC), consider the following statements:
1. Subhash Chandra Bose was elected as the President of the Haripura session with the support of Gandhi.
2. Pattabhi Sittaramaiyya won the elections for the President of the Tripuri session after defeating Subhash Chandra Bose with Gandhi’s backing and support.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation:
In 1938, Netaji Subhas Chandra Bose was elected as party President during the Indian National Congress's 58th annual session in Haripura, Gujarat. His victory was mainly due to the support of most senior Congress leaders such as Mahatma Gandhi, Jawaharlal Nehru and Vallabhbhai Patel. So, statement 1 is correct.
Towards the end of the Haripura session, there was a contest for the President-elect for the next session (1939 at Tripuri) between Netaji and Pattabhi Sittaramayya. Bose won with a margin even though Sittaramayya was seen as Gandhi's nominee. So, statement 2 is not correct.
Even Gandhi regarded this defeat as his defeat. There were problems in the formation of the working committee and ultimately Bose resigned from the Presidentship.
Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
With reference to the ‘Anti-Compromise Conference’, consider the following statements:
1. It was convened by Mohan Singh under the joint auspices of the Forward Bloc and Kisan Sabha.
2. It decided to not cooperate with the British during World War II either with men, money or materials.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation:
On his resignation from the Congress presidency, Subhash Chandra Bose formed ‘Forward Bloc’ in 1939 as a radical faction within the framework of the Congress. The objective behind the formation of the new party was to rally all radical and anti-imperialist progressive elements in the country.
In 1940, Subhash Chandra Bose convened an ‘Anti-Compromise Conference’ at Ramgarh in Bihar under the joint auspices of the Forward Bloc and Kisan Sabha. Thus, it was not convened by Mohan Singh. So, statement 1 is not correct.
In the Anti-Compromise Conference, a resolution was made to stubbornly oppose the exploitation of Indian resources to preserve the interests of the British Empire. It was decided in the conference that a worldwide struggle should be initiated in April 1940 to urge the people not to cooperate with the British during World War II either with men, money or materials. So, statement 2 is correct.
Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
Consider the following events with reference to Indian history:
1. Formation of Indian Home Rule Leagues
2. August Declaration
3. Launch of Non-Cooperation Movement and Khilafat Movements
4. Establishment of the Hindu Mahasabha by Madan Mohan Malaviya
5. Foundation of the Women’s University at Poona
How many of the above events took place during the tenure of Viceroy Chelmsford?
explanation:
Chelmsford served as a Viceroy of India from 1916 to 1921. He was a pioneer for legislation of the Government of India Act 1919 which is also known as the Montagu–Chelmsford Reforms 1919. Some of the landmark events that occurred during his tenure are as follows:
Formation of the Indian Home Rule League (1916): The movement under this League lasted around two years between 1916 and 1918. So, point 1 is correct.
August Declaration (1917): This Montagu declaration gave the provision of a responsible government. As per the declaration, the control over the Indian Government would be gradually transferred to the Indian people. So, point 2 is correct.
The Non-Cooperation Movement (1920) and Khilafat Movements were launched during his tenure. So, point 3 is correct.
Foundation of Women’s University (1916): Women’s universities in India as well as in South-East Asia were founded by Maharshi Dr. Dhondo Keshav Karve in 1916 at Poona. So, point 5 is correct.
Hindu Mahasabha (1915) by Madan Mohan Malaviya was established during the tenure of Viceroy Lord Hardinge II (1910-1916). So, point 4 is not correct.
So, only four of the above events took place during the tenure of Viceroy Chelmsford.
Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
With reference to the differences in the working of the Moderates and Extremists during India’s freedom struggle:
1. Unlike Moderates, Extremists rejected the ‘providential mission theory’ as an illusion.
2. The social base of moderates included zamindars while that of extremists included educated middle classes.
3. Unlike Moderates, Extremists did not reject the use of violent methods against British Rule.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
explanation:
Moderates believed in England’s providential mission in India. They believed political connections with Britain to be in India’s social, political and cultural interests. On the other hand, Extremists rejected the ‘providential mission theory’ as an illusion. They believed that political connections with Britain would perpetuate British exploitation of India. So, statement 1 is correct.
As per the theory, it was believed that British conquests in India had a providential purpose and that imperial Britain had been called by God to Christianize India through an alliance of Church and empire.
The social base of moderates included zamindars and upper middle classes in towns. In contrast, the social base of extremists included educated middle and lower-middle classes in towns. So, statement 2 is correct.
Moderate leaders insisted on the use of constitutional methods only. The Extremist leaders disfavoured the use of violence against British rule and did not approve of the methods of political murder and assassination used by the Indian revolutionaries. However, the extremist leaders did not hesitate to use extra-constitutional methodslike boycotts and passive resistance to achieve their objectives. So, statement 3 is not correct.
Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
Consider the following statements:
1. The Indian Councils Act of 1892 allowed a non-official majority in the Provincial Legislative Councils.
2. The Government of India Act of 1909 extended the principle of communal representation to depressed classes, women and labour.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation:
The Indian Councils Act of 1892 increased the number of additional (non-official) members in the Central and Provincial Legislative Councils but maintained the official majority in them. It was the Indian Councils Act of 1909 that retained an official majority in the Central Legislative Council but allowed the Provincial Legislative Councils to have a non-official majority. So, statement 1 is not correct.
The separate electorates/communal electorates were introduced through the Government of India Act of 1909. The principle of communal representation was extended with separate electorates for Sikhs, Christians and Anglo-Indians, besides Muslims through the Government of India Act of 1919. The principles of communal electorates and ‘weightage’ were further extended to depressed classes, women and labour through the Government of India Act of 1935. So, statement 2 is not correct.
Therefore, option (d) is the answer.
subject: History

questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Sl. No. Organisation Associated Leader
1. All India Women’s Conference : Margaret Cousins
2. National Council of Women in India : Mehribai Tata
3. Indian National Trade Union Congress (INTUC) : Acharya JB Kripalani
4. All India Trade Union Congress (AITUC) : Dewan Chaman Lal
How many pairs given above are correctly matched?
explanation:
The All India Women’s Conference (AIWC) was founded by Margaret Cousins in 1927. The objective of the conference was to work for a society based on principles of social justice, integrity, equal rights and opportunities. The important founding members of the conference include Maharani Chimnabai Gaekwad, Rani Sahiba of Sangli, Sarojini Naidu, Kamla Devi Chattopadhyaya and Lady Dorab Tata. So, pair 1 is correctly matched.
The National Council of Women in India was formed as a national branch of the International Council of Women in 1925. Mehribai Tata played a vital role in its formation and advancement. She opined that the purdah system, caste differences and lack of education prevented women from working to solve societal problems. So, pair 2 is correctly matched.
Indian National Trade Union Congress (INTUC) was born on May 3, 1947, just before Indian attained independence. It aimed to voice the genuine demands for realising the aspirations of the working class in the country, at the same time keeping the national interest foremost in view. Acharya JB Kripalani, the incumbent President of the Indian National Congress, presided over the inaugural session of the founding conference of INTUC. So, pair 3 is correctly matched.
The All India Trade Union Congress (AITUC) was founded in 1920. Lala Lajpat Rai was elected as the first president of the All India Trade Union Congress and Dewan Chaman Lal as the first general secretary. So, pair 4 is correctly matched.
So, all four of the above pairs are correctly matched.
Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
Consider the following movements:
1. Non-cooperation Movement
2. Civil Disobedience Movement
3. Quit India Movement
How many of the above movements resulted in the Indian National Congress being declared illegal by the British government?
explanation:
During 1919-22, the British were opposed through the Khilafat and Non-Cooperation movements. Though the two movements emerged from separate issues, they adopted a common programme of action that of non-violent non-cooperation. The Indian National Congress was not declared illegal during the Non-cooperation movement. So, point 1 is not correct.
During the Civil Disobedience Movement, a series of repressive ordinances were issued by the British Government. Congress organisations at all levels were banned and the Indian National Congress was declared as illegal. So, point 2 is correct.
During the Quit India Movement, the Congress Working Committee, the All India Congress Committee and the Provincial Congress Committees were declared unlawful associations under the Criminal Law Amendment Act of 1908. However, the Indian National Congress as a whole was not declared illegal. So, point 3 is not correct.
So, only one of the above movements resulted in the Indian National Congress being declared illegal by the British Government.
Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText: During British rule, the ‘Simla Deputation’ was related to which one of the following?
options: a) Separate electorates for the Muslims
b) Formation of the Indian National Congress
c) Advocacy for complete independence from British rule
d) Opposition to the Simon Commission
explanation:
Simla Deputation organized by the Indian Muslim leaders, met the Governor General and Viceroy Lord Minto in Simla on 1 October 1906. The deputation aimed to win the sympathies of the Raj on their side concerning matters relating to their interests as a community. It also demanded separate electorates for the Muslims.
The demands the deputation put forward to lord Minto were:
employment of Muslims to Civil administration, the military and the Judiciary in sufficient numbers; no competitive examinations should bar employment to the higher posts
preservation of a certain number of seats for Muslims in the municipal and district boards and in the university senate and syndicate
separate election of Muslims to the Provincial council based on their political importance and not on the population ratio
election of a sufficient number of Muslims to the Imperial Legislative Council to avoid reducing Muslims to an insignificant minority
establishment of a Muslim University which would stand as a glory to the religious and cultural life of the Muslims
In response Lord Minto expressed his indirect support to the principal demand of the Muslim leaders as enunciated in the memorandum, e.g., a separate electorate for the Muslims. This showed the success of the deputation. Eventually, in 1909, the Morley-Minto Reforms made provisions for a separate electorate for the Muslims of India.
Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
Which of the following were the demands of Moderates during the early phase of the Indian National Congress?
1. Reduction in land revenue and the abolition of salt tax
2. Increase in expenditure on health, sanitation and education
3. Reduction in military expenditure
4. Indianisation of government service
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
explanation:
The early nationalists were led by Dadabhai Naoroji, R.C. Dutt, Dinshaw Wacha and others carefully analysed the political economy of British rule in India. They demanded the development of an independent economy through the involvement of Indian capital and enterprise. The demands of the early nationalists are as follows:
Reduction in land revenue, abolition of salt tax and policies to help in the growth of Indian industries and handicrafts. So, point 1 is correct.
Call for an increase in expenditure on welfare (i.e. health, sanitation), education and Demand for better treatment for Indian labour abroad in other British colonies (especially in Africa), where they faced oppression and racial discrimination. So, point 2 is correct.
Reduction in military expenditure, encouragement to modern industry through tariff protection and direct government aid. So, point 3 is correct.
Indianisation of government service, protection of civil rights, call for separation of judicial from executive functions. So, point 4 is correct.
Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference to the history of Indian National Congress (INC):
1. The majority of the delegates in the inaugural session of INC were from Bengal province.
2. The Indian Social Conference was popularly known as the social reform cell of the INC.
3. The President of an INC session was elected from the province where the session was held.
How many of the above statements are correct?
explanation:
The first session of the Indian National Congress was organised in Bombay in December 1885. It was attended by 72 delegates and presided over by Womesh Chandra Bonnerjee. Of the 72 non-official Indian representatives who attended the first Congress session, 38 came from Bombay, 21 from Madras, and only four from Bengal. The three towns of Punjab sent a representative each, and the four principal towns of the North-Western Provinces and Awadh sent seven members. Leaders from Bengal were apprised of the Congress session at the very last moment. So, statement 1 is not correct.
Founded by M.G. Ranade and Raghunath Rao, the Indian Social Conference met annually from its first session in Madras in 1887 at the same time and venue as the Indian National Congress. It focused attention on the social issues of importance; it could be called the social reform cell of the Indian National Congress. So, statement 2 is correct.
In the annual sessions of the INC, a leader from the host province could not become the president of the session. That’s why in 1907, Bal Gangadhar Tilak could not become the President as Surat was situated in his home province of Bombay. So, statement 3 is not correct.
So, only one of the above statements is correct.
Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference to Surya Sen:
1. He joined Anushilan Samiti in 1916.
2. He believed that a revolutionary must be inspired by the ideals of Humanism.
3. He commanded the 1930 Chittagong armoury raid.
How many of the above statements is/are correct?
explanation:
Statement 1 is correct: Surya Sen was selected as the President of the Chittagong District Congress Committee in 1918. He also took part in the noncooperation movement and continued working for congress for some time. While in college, he was influenced by revolutionary nationalist ideas. He then joined Anushilan Samiti in 1916.
Statement 2 is correct: Surya Sen always used to say “Humanism is a special virtue of a revolutionary”. He believed that the ideals of Humanism must guide the activities of a revolutionary even in the path of violent movement in the cause of freedom for the motherland.
Statement 3 is correct: Surya Sen is remembered for having commanded the 1930 Chittagong armoury raid. He actively participated in the Non-Cooperation Movement before being detained and held in prison for about two years (1926-1928) due to his revolutionary activities. The raid was quite successful and he hoisted the national flag and proclaimed a provisional revolutionary government.
Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference to Vinayak Damodar Savarkar:
1. He founded the Free India Society in 1906 to fight for Indian Independence.
2. He defended the caste system along with Chaturvarna and gave a scientific basis for it.
3. He wrote books on Joseph Mazzini and the Revolt of 1857.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
explanation:
Vinayak Damodar Savarkar, also known as Swatantryaveer Savarkar, was an Indian independence activist, politician, lawyer and writer. He founded the Free India Society in 1906 to fight for Indian Independence from the Britishers. In May 1904, he also established an International Revolution Institute named ‘Abhinav Bharat’. So, statement 1 is correct.
Savarkar was a critic of the caste system. He held that both 'Chaturvarna' and the caste system proved very disastrous for the unity of Hindu society. He held that 'Chaturvarna' was not based on any scientific criterion, but was a creation of scriptures that gave birth to the inhuman practice of untouchability. So, statement 2 is not correct.
He wrote the book “Joseph Mazzini- Biography and Politics” and also published “The Indian War of Independence” about the Indian rebellion of 1857. So, statement 3 is correct.
Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
What is the correct chronological order for the following events in the history of Modern India?
1. Passage of Rowlatt act
2. Non-cooperation movement
3. Jallianwala Bagh massacre
4. Komagata Maru Incident
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
explanation:
Option 4: The SS Komagata Maru was a chartered ship featured in a dramatic challenge to Canada’s former practice of excluding immigrants from India. This challenge took place in the spring and summer of 1914, on the eve of the First World War. It proved to be a bitter and tragic experience for the passengers, first in an unsuccessful and eventually physical confrontation with officials, police and the military at the Port of Vancouver, and then in a deadly encounter with police and troops near Kolkata on the passengers’ return to India.
Option 1: The enactment of the Rowlatt Act in March 1919 marked a significant erosion of civil liberties in India. Under this law, political activists could be subjected to trial without juries and even detained without trial based solely on suspicion of treason. Furthermore, individuals could be arrested without warrants, tried in secret, and denied access to legal assistance, effectively suspending the fundamental principle of habeas corpus.
Option 3: The tragic incident known as the Jallianwala Bagh massacre occurred on April 13, 1919, in Amritsar, Punjab. A large peaceful gathering had assembled at Jallianwala Bagh to voice their opposition to the Rowlatt Act and the arrest of prominent pro-independence activists Saifuddin Kitchlew and Satyapal. Responding to this gathering, Brigadier General R. E. H. Dyer and his regiment surrounded the protesters and ordered open fire on the unarmed crowd.
Option 2:The Non-Cooperation Movement was officially launched on August 31, 1920, following the initiation of a campaign by the Khilafat Committee. In a special session held in Calcutta in 1920, the Indian National Congress endorsed a non-cooperation program aimed at addressing the injustices in Punjab and Khilafat affairs and achieving swaraj, or self-rule.
Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
With reference to the Hunter Committee of 1919, consider the following statements:
1. Its sole purpose was to investigate the Jallianwala Bagh massacre.
2. The committee did not include any Indian as a member.
3. The final report of this committee unanimously condemned General Dyer’s action.
4. It recommended the removal of Dyer from his post.
Which of the statement/s given above is/are correct?
explanation:
The purpose of the Hunter Committee was not only to investigate the Jallianwala Bagh massacre but also to investigate the recent disturbances in Bombay, Delhi and Punjab, about their causes, and the measures taken to cope with them. Hence, Statement 1 is incorrect.
Composition: There were three Indian members in the Hunter committee, namely, Sir Chamanlal Harilal Setalvad, Vice-Chancellor of Bombay University and advocate of the Bombay High Court; Pandit Jagat Narayan, lawyer and Member of the Legislative Council of the United Provinces; and Sardar Sahibzada Sultan Ahmad Khan, lawyer from Gwalior State. Hence, Statement 2 is incorrect.
The final report, released in March 1920, unanimously condemned Dyer’s actions. The report stated that the lack of notice to disperse from the Bagh in the beginning was an error; the length of firing showed a grave error; Dyer’s motive of producing a sufficient moral effect was to be condemned; Dyer had overstepped the bounds of his authority; there had been no conspiracy to overthrow British rule in the Punjab. Hence, Statement 3 is correct.
The committee did not recommend removing Dyer from his post. He was removed because the British cabinet decided so. Churchill observed that Dyer was a dangerous man and could not be allowed to continue in his post. The decision that Dyer should be dismissed was conveyed to the Army Council. In the end, Dyer was found guilty of a mistaken notion of duty and relieved of his command in March 1920. He was recalled to England. No legal action was taken against him; he drew half pay and received his army pension. Hence, Statement 4 is incorrect.
Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText: Which of the following is correct regarding the Swadeshi Movement in India?
options: a) Mass participation by Indian peasantry was a notable feature of the movement.
b) Gaffar khan organised ‘khudai khidmatgar’ in support of the movement.
c) The zamindari and lower middle class of cities participated in the movement.
d) The Tilak Swaraj Fund was established to raise funds for the Swadeshi Movement.
explanation:
One of the drawbacks of the swadeshi movement was that it failed to reach Indian peasantry. Although there was participation by untouched sections like students and women. The movement largely remained confined to the upper and middle classes and zamindars. The social base was restricted to cities only. Hence, Statement a is incorrect.
Gaffar khan had organised a volunteer brigade ‘khudai khidmatgar’ or Red-Shirts among the pashtuns somewhere around 1929. It was in the Civil disobedience movement that this force showed a significant response. They pledged to participate in the national movement in a non-violent way. Hence, Statement b is incorrect.
The social base of the movement included sections of the zamindari, the students, the women, and the lower middle classes in cities and towns. An attempt was also made to politicise the issues of the working class by organising strikes. But the movement was not able to get support of the Muslims, especially the Muslim peasantry. Upper and middle class Muslims stayed away. Hence, Statement c is correct.
The Tilak Swaraj Fund was established during the Non-Cooperation Movement to raise funds for the Non-Cooperation Movement. In the Nagpur session of Congress which was held in December 1921, a decision was taken to create the All-India Tilak Swaraj Fund (in the memory of Bal Gangadhar Tilak) to raise funds for the Non-Cooperation Movement. This Tilak Swaraj Fund was oversubscribed and one crore rupees collected. Hence, Statement d is incorrect.
Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
With reference to the factors that led to the Home Rule League Movement in India, consider the following statements:
1. Morley-Minto reforms failed to provide constitutional reforms.
2. High taxation and price rise due to the first world war had inflicted difficulty on the masses.
3. The myth of white superiority was exposed by the World War.
4. It was felt that popular pressure was required to attain concessions from the government.
How many of the above statements are correct?
explanation:
The Home Rule Movement was started in 1916. It was based along the lines of the Irish Home Rule League. The movement was an Indian response to the First World War. It was less aggressive but more effective than the Ghadr adventure. This movement shifted the emphasis from educated elites to masses. Annie Beasant and Tilak were pioneers of this new age of aggressive politics.
Lord Morley was against the introduction of responsible government as demanded by congress in India. The constitutional reforms introduced in Government of India Act 1909 (Morley- Minto reforms) disappointed the congress especially moderates. Therefore, demand for self-rule was made the aim for the Home Rule League Movement. Under it, administration reforms were demanded along the lines of the Irish home rule league. Hence, Statement 1 is correct.
Home rule started during the first world war time (1914-1919). As Britain was part of the first world war, people in India were facing wartime difficulties. There was a high price rise and high taxation was imposed on them. They were ready to participate in any aggressive movement of protest. Hence, Statement 2 is correct.
One of the factors which contributed to the formation of the Home Rule movement was the World war, which exposed the myth of white superiority. Hence, Statement 3 is correct.
One of the reasons which contributed to the formation of the Home Rule league was that a section of the nationalists felt that popular pressure was required to attain concessions from the government. Hence, Statement 4 is correct.
Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
With reference to the National Council of Education, consider the following statements:
1. The National Council of Education was an outcome of the Swadeshi movement.
2. It recommended that the medium of education in schools and colleges should be English.
3. It had set up the Bengal Technical Institute for imparting technical education.
How many of the above statements is/are correct?
explanation:
The National Council of Education was set up in June, 1906 by Satish Chandra Mukherjee and other Indian nationalists. Its main pur-pose was to provide education on national lines and under national control.
The birth of the national schools during the swadeshi movement culminated in the formation of the National Council of Education (NCE) in 1906. Hence, Statement 1 is correct.
The medium of education was recommended to be vernacular. The Council was started in the backdrop of the Swadeshi and Boycott Movement when British goods were being boycotted. Hence, Statement 2 is incorrect.
Bengal Technical Institute was not started by the National Council of Education but its sister organization-the Society for the Promotion of Technical Education (SPTE) on 25th July, 1906. Hence, Statement 3 is incorrect.
Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText: Which of the following was the reason for Indian objections to Montagu’s Statement of 1917?
options: a) The statement failed to provide any plan for transfer of power to Indians.
b) It was aimed at rallying Muslims against Congress by providing a separate electorate to Muslims.
c) The British government alone would decide on the nature and the timing of forming a responsible government in India.
d) Demand of Congress to vote upon the budget was not granted under Montagu’s statement.
explanation:
In August, 1917, The Secretary of State for India, Edwin Samuel Montagu, made a statement in the British House of Commons. It later came to be known as the August Declaration of 1917. The statement said: “The government policy is of an increasing participation of Indians in every branch of administration and gradual development of self-governing institutions with a view to the progressive realisation of responsible government in India as an integral part of the British Empire.”
It was after Cripps Mission in 1942, when there was the objection made regarding absence of any plan for immediate transfer of power and absence of any real share in defence. Hence, Option a is incorrect.
The Morley-Minto reforms in 1909 provided separate electorates for Muslims. It was aimed at appeasing the Muslim section of population to check the popularity of Congress. Hence, option b is incorrect.
The objections of the Indians to Montagu’s statement were two-fold:
No specific time frame was provided to form responsible government on the lines of self-rule.
The British government alone would decide the nature and the timing of progress towards a responsible government. The Indians were displeased that the British would decide good and bad for Indians. Hence, option c is correct.
After Indian Councils Act 1892, congress and nationalists were dissatisfied with the reforms. They demanded a majority of elected Indians in council and control over the budget in the form of voting upon and amending the budget. Hence, option d is incorrect.
Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
With reference to Rowlatt Satyagraha, consider the following statements:
1. The Rowlatt Satyagraha was the first all India mass Satyagraha called by Gandhi.
2. It was mostly restricted to cities and towns.
3. Members of the Home rule league stayed away from the satyagraha.
How many of the above statements are correct?
explanation:
In March 1919, Gandhi initiated a nationwide Satyagraha against the Rowlatt Act, marking his inaugural endeavor to lead a mass Satyagraha across India. The chosen forms of protest included organizing a nationwide strike (hartal), followed by fasting, prayer, civil disobedience against specific laws, and willingly accepting arrest and imprisonment. Hence, Statement 1 is correct.
A notable limitation of the Rowlatt Satyagraha was its predominantly urban focus, failing to garner uniform response throughout India. However, despite limited organizational backing, various classes and communities heeded Gandhi's call for action. Hence, Statement 2 is correct.
Members of the Home Rule League were in support of the Rowlatt Satyagraha. Gandhi convened a Satyagraha Sabha, convincing younger members of the Home Rule Leagues and Pan Islamists to participate in the movement. Initiatives by these league members, alongside those of the All-India Muslim League, helped raise awareness about the British Government's actions. Hence, Statement 3 is incorrect.
Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText: ‘Anjuman-i-Mohisban-i-Watan’was a secret society formed to support the agitations against the Punjab Land Colonisation Bill. Who among the following personalities is the founder of this secret society?
explanation:
The 1907 Punjab unrests were a period of unrest in the British Indian province of Punjab, centered on the province’s Colonization bill, which was implemented in 1906. In response to Colonization bill, Ajit singh formed a secret society, known by the name Anjuman-i-Mohisban-i-Watan.
The Colonization Bill provided the transfer of a person’s property to the government after his death if he had no heirs. The government could sell the property to any public or private developer. This was utterly contrary to the social conditions in the region, and thus it was rejected by all parties. During this time, there were widespread protests, which culminated in Ajit Singh’s deportation.
Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
Consider the following statements regarding the Ghadar Party and its programmes:
1. The first President of Ghadar party was Lala Hardayal.
2. The first issue of Ghadar Paper was in Urdu.
3. The revolutionaries of Ghadar included mainly ex-soldiers and peasants.
4. The Ghadar activities were largely based on religious lines.
How many of the above statements are correct?
explanation:
Baba Sohan Singh Bhakna was elected the President, Lala Har Dayal the General Secretary and Pandit Kanshi Ram Maroli the Treasurer at the first meeting of the Party. Hence, Statement 1 is incorrect.
The paper Ghadar was launched on the first of November, 1913. The first issue was in Urdu language followed by the Gurmukhi version, a month later. Hence, Statement 2 is correct.
The revolutionaries included mainly ex-soldiers and peasants who had migrated from the Punjab to the USA and Canada in search of better employment opportunities. Hence, Statement 3 is correct.
The Ghadar ideology espoused strong egalitarian and democratic principles, aiming to establish an independent republic in India. A significant accomplishment of the Ghadarites was their steadfast secular outlook, despite recruiting primarily from among Punjabi Sikh immigrants. Hence, Statement 4 is incorrect.
Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.
subject: History

questionText:
With reference to Jallianwala Bagh Incident, consider the following statements:
1. Mahatma Gandhi renounced his ‘knighthood’ to protest the Jallianwala Bagh Massacre.
2. Saifuddin Kitchlew and Satyapal were arrested immediately after the Jallianwala Bagh incident.
3. People gathered in Jallianwala Bagh to celebrate the Baisakhi festival.
Which of the above statements is/are correct?
explanation:
The Jallianwala Bagh massacre or the Amritsar massacre of 13th April 1919 accounts for the gruesome execution of hundreds of innocent people on the orders of the Brigadier R.E.H. Dyer.
Jallianwala Bagh became a key point in the history of India’s struggle for independence and it is now an important monument in the country. The Bengali poet and Nobel laureate Rabindranath Tagore renounced the knighthood that he had received in 1915. Gandhi gave up the title of Kaiser-i-Hind. Hence, Statement 1 is incorrect.
Mahatma Gandhi wanted non-violent civil disobedience against such unjust laws, which would start with a hartal on 6th April 1919. In Punjab, on 9th April 1919, two nationalist leaders, Saifuddin Kitchlew and Dr. Satyapal, were arrested by the British officials without any provocation. This caused resentment among the Indian protestors who came out in thousands on 10th April to show their solidarity with their leaders. Thus, these 2 leaders were arrested before the Jallianwala bagh incident. Hence, Statement 2 is incorrect.
Statement 3 is correct: On Baisakhi day, a large crowd of people mostly from neighbouring villages, gathered in the Jallianwala Bagh, to celebrate the Baisakhi festival. Local leaders had also called for a protest meeting at the venue. Hence, Statement 3 is correct.
Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.
subject: History
